title: Julia Basics & VAR Process
text:
NoteObjectives






Introduce Julia environment

run cells
basic types: numbers, strings, vectors, matrices
functions
simple plots

Simulate AR1 models

impulse response functions
stochastic simulations

conditional / unconditional moments

develop intuition about eigenvalues / ergodic distributions

title: Julia Basics & VAR Process
text:
Why Julia?
Developped at MIT on top of opensource technologies

linux / git / llvm

Syntax inspired by Matlab but:

more consistent
lots of features from high level languages

Everything is JIT-compiled

no interpreted vs compiled treadeoff -> very fast
most of the base library is written in Julia

Opensource/free + vibrant community

# mutating vectors
x = ["One"]
push!(x, "Two")

push!(x, "Five")

# Note how the type of the vector is different from above

Warning



Indexing in Julia is 1-based. First element of a collection is denoted by 1.

println(M)

# keep first line

println(M[1,:])

title: Opening Economy Extension
text:
NoteObjectives






Modify a modfile with a model extension.
Compare model solutions for different parameterizations.










TipModfiles














rbc_fixed.mod











rbc_open.mod











rbc_open_stationary.mod











rbc_open_stationary_kappa05.mod





No matching items




Uncomment the following lines to install needed packages (if not already present).

# uncomment the next lines, the first time you run
# import Pkg; Pkg.add(["DBnomics", "Dynare", "DataFrames"])


Small Open Economy
Start from the same rbc model. Assume the representative agent can save \(b^{\star}_t\) as foreign assets, remunerated at a constant interest rate \(r^{\star}-1\).
Write the new budget constraint for the representative household.






WarningCorrection





At given date t, the household saves \(b^{\star}_t\) in the foreigh country and receives repayment \(b^{\star}_{t-1}\) plus interests \((r^{\star} вИТ 1)b^{\star}_{t-1}\) . Starting from the RBC constraint, the new constraint becomes: \[c_t + i_t + b^ {\star}_t \leq w_t n_t + r_t k_{t-1} + r^ {\star} b^{\star}_{t-1}\]
and the lagrangian is:
\[\mathcal{L} = E_t \sum_{j=0}^\infty \beta^j\left[\log c_{t+j} + \chi \dfrac{(1-n_{t+j})^{1-\eta}}{1-\eta} \right.\]
\[+\lambda_{t+j} (w_{t+j}n_{t+j} + r_{t+j}k_{t+j-1} - c_{t+j}-k_{t+j} + (1-\delta)k_{t+j-1} + r^* b^*_{t+j-1} - b^*_{t+j} )\Bigg]\]



Write the new optimality condition.






WarningCorrection





We must add the optimality condition relative to \(b^{\star}_t\) : the representative household chooses amount of savings to maximise its intertemporal utility of consumption. We find :
\[ -\lambda_t + \beta E_t[\lambda_{t+1}r^*]=0\] or : \[ \frac{1}{c_t}=\beta r^* E_t\left[\frac{1}{c_{t+1}} \right]\]



What is the long run constraint on interest rate \(r^{\star}\)?






WarningCorrection





In the deterministic steady-state the euler equation implies \(r^{\star}= \frac{1}{\beta}\) . In principle \(r^{\star}\) is a a foreign interest rate determined by factors external to the country. Yet \(\frac{1}{\beta}\) is the only value it can take that is compatible with the existence of a long run steady-state. This can be interpreted as an arbitrage condition: \(\frac{1}{\beta}\) is the domestic intesrest rate. If it was lower/higher than world interest rate, there would be infinite capital flows from/to the country.
(in a deterministic setting the arbitrage principle implies that all investment opportunities must yield the same return)



Update the modfile (set \(\overline{b^{\star}}=0\))






WarningCorrection





To update the modfile, one must declare a new variable \(b^{\star}\) and the new parameter \(r^\star\). The latter is set to \(\frac{1}{\beta}\) . One must also add the above optimality condition and update the equation characterizing the equilibrium on the goods markets. That one comes from the budget constraint. Rewriting the budget constraint for a small open economy we have:
\[c_t + i_t + b^{\star}_t = w_t n_t + r_t k_{t-1} + r^{\star} b^{\star}_{t-1}\]
Since we assume competitive markets, the firm doesnвАЩt do any profit. We have \(y_t = w_t n_t + r_t k_{tвИТ1}\) and:
\[c_t + i_t + b^{\star}_t - r^{\star} b^{\star}_{t-1} = y_t\]
Next, we can observe that the static model doesnвАЩt pin down the steady-state level of \(b^{\star}\) ; we set it to 0, so that the steady-state values of all other variables ar unchanged. We thus add \(b^{\star}=0\) in the steady_state_model; block.




using Dynare

context = @dynare "rbc_open.mod";
# saving the result in context is useful for future comparison

We can see that Dynare cannot compute the moments (mean and standard deviation) for all variables. This is a property associated to the existence of a unit root: the ergodic distribution of this variables is undefined.
We say the model is non-stationary.



Simulate the model over 100 periods. Comment.






WarningCorrection





The simulation horizon is specified in the stoch_simul(irf=200, order=1) as the argument to the irf keyword. The result is stored in the rbc_open/graphs subfolder.
Note that dynare-matlab allows you to select which variables to plot by adding a list of variables after the command (as in stoch_simul(irf=200, order=1) y c i b). This functionality has not been ported yet to Dynare Julia which prints by default the 8 first variables. You can instead

reorder the variables in the declaration statement to list first the ones we want to print
make a custom plot with Plots.jl (we do in the last question)


Even with a unit root, the model can be solved and simulated.
Since the opening up of the economy, in response to the productivity shock capital flows in to finance a jump in investment.
The irfs show a persistent effect of the shock on the debt position, as well as most other variables.
When the savings of all households increase, there is no stabilizing force to bring them back to their original level.
Households and as a result the whole country behaves like a ricardian agent: they increase their consumption permanently by consuming the interests on their assets.
Since their consumption increase their consumption leisure tradeoff is also modified, which is why the labour supply is also permanently changed.
Since the return on capital must be kept equal to world interest rate (by the arbitrage condition), it returns to the steady-state after one period.



Assume the foreign interest rate depends on the amount of foreign assets
\[r^{\star}_t=\frac{1}{\beta} + exp(-\kappa b^{\star}_t) - 1\]
with \(\kappa=0.001\). How do you interpret the equation for \(r^{\star}_t\). How should you modify the model equations?






WarningCorrection





With this specification, when the representative housheold holds more assets, their return decrease. This could be a proxy for the fact that international markets have limited depth.
Conversely, when the domestic household become more indebted, international lenders require a higher interest rate. In that case, it could mean that international investors donвАЩt have infinitely deep pockets or interpreted as a higher risk premium.
The precise specification is also chosen so that it collapses to the \(r^{\star}=\frac{1}{\beta}\) when \(b^{\star}_t=0\). This allows us to keep essentially the same steady-state as before for all other variables.
To update the modfile, we need to declare \(r^{\star}\) as a variable not a parameter and move its steady-state value \(r^{\star} = \frac{1}{ќ≤}\) to the block steady_state_model;. Finally the above equation endogenizing the interest rate must augment the model; block.
Note that the Euler equation is unchanged by the addition of the endogenous interest rate. This is because the households (and as a result the representative household) does not internalize the effect of its borrowing/saving on the interest rate when it chooses its optimal decisions.



Update the modfile and comment on the generated moments. Do they depend on the choice of \(\kappa\)?

using Dynare 
#run modified modfile rbc_open_stationary
# save result for future comparison
context_stationary = @dynare "rbc_open_stationary.mod";

We observe rather high increase in the volatility of debt, but milder changes for capital, investment, output, labour, wagesвА¶ In particular the RBC moments (std dev of investment / consumption over std dev of production) move very little.
Another interesting exercise consists in comparing the impulse response functions obtained with \(\kappa=0.000\), \(\kappa=0.001\) and \(\kappa=0.005\) (see code below).
In the short run, the dynamic responses are very similar for all variables, except consumption which does not jump anymore to its long run trend as long as there is a small friction on the interest rate. Overall the addition of the friction slows down the adjustment of production variables to the productivty shock.
In the long run, the friction implies a return to the initial steady-state counteracting the short term effect of the exogenous shock.

Small Open Economy
Start from the same rbc model as in last tutorial session. Assume the representative agent can save \(b^{\star}_t\) as foreign assets, remunerated at a constant interest rate \(r^{\star}-1\).
Write the new budget constraint for the representative household.
Write the new optimality condition.
What is the long run constraint on interest rate \(r^{\star}\)?
Update the modfile (set \(\overline{b^{\star}}=0\))
Print theoretical moments. Comment.
Simulate the model over 100 periods. Comment.
Assume the foreign interest rate depends on the amount of foreign assets
\[r^{\star}_t=\frac{1}{\beta} + exp(-\kappa b^{\star}_t) - 1\]
with \(\kappa=0.001\). How do you interpret the equation for \(r^{\star}_t\). How should you modify the model equations?
Update the modfile and comment on the generated moments. Do they depend on the choice of \(\kappa\)?

title: A two agents model of inequalities.
section: Preference for wealth and marginal propensity to consume
text:
Preference for wealth and marginal propensity to consume
For now, we consider a single representative agent. She has the ability to buy a two periods bond, yielding 1 after one period. The price of the bond at any date is \(q\), hence its (riskfree) interest rate is \(r=1/q\).
Agent values consumption \(c_t\) and wealth \(b_t q_t\) so that she maximizes1:
\[\max \sum_t \beta^t \left( \frac{c_t^{1-\frac{1}{\sigma}}}{1-\frac{1}{\sigma}}+ \varphi \frac{ (1+b_t)^{1-\frac{1}{\eta}} } {1-\frac{1}{\eta}} \right)\]
under the budget constraint
\[c_t = y_t + b_{t-1} - b_t q_t\]
where \(y_t\) is exogenous income following AR1
\[(y_t-\overline{y})=\rho (y_{t-1}-\overline{y}) + \epsilon^y_t\]

using Dynare


Write down the optimality condition for debt holdings.







WarningCorrection





We get:
\[q_t = \beta \left(\frac{c_{t+1}}{c_{t}}\right)^{-\frac{1}{\sigma}}+\varphi \left(\frac{1 + b_t q_t}{c_{t}}\right)^{-\frac{1}{\sigma}}\]



What are the equations defining the deterministic equilibrium?






WarningCorrection





At the equilibium, the last equation becomes. \[q = \beta + \varphi \left(\frac{1 + b q}{c}\right)^{-\frac{1}{\sigma}}\]



3. Inspect and run one_agent.mod model. Show that there is a unit root. Can you interpret it?






WarningCorrection





The one_agent.mod model is a standard consumption saving model without preference for wealth.
In general, if the solution of a model is \(y_t = A y_{t-1}\) where \(y_t\) is the vector of variables, we have a unit root when one of the eigenvalues of A is equal to 1 in absolute terms.
In the specific case of the modfile, we can directly read the values of matrix \(A\) are given in the output of dynare (precisely it is a subset of \(A'\))
  Coefficients of approximate solution function (reduced form)

вФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФђвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФА
       вФВ y_t   c_t    b_t
вФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФА
  ѕХ(b) вФВ 0.0  0.0385  1.0
 e_y_t вФВ 0.1  0.0038  0.1
вФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФівФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФА
The rows correspond to the (predetermined) states. Non predetermined variables do not appear as rows (or equivalently with zeros everywhere).
Here we see that \(b_t\) is the only endogenous state. We also see that its dynamic is given by:
\[b_t = 1.0 b_{t-1} + 0.1 e_{y,t}\]
In other words, the dynamic of \(b\) is an AR1 with perfect persistence.
This behaviour (like in an open economy) comes from the fact that any steady-state level of debt is feasible (in a deterministic model). Hence shocks can affect the steady-state permanently.

What is the consumption response to a temporary income shock? To a permanent one? (with autocorrelation \(\rho=0.9\) and \(\rho=1.0\))







WarningCorrection





Change the income equation:

for a temporary shock remove the zero to get: y = ybar + e_y
For a persistent one, define a new parameter in the parameters line initialized to rho=0.9 and change the equation to y - ybar = rho*(y(-1) - ybar) + e_y. In the case rho=1.0 note the apparition of a second unit root.

In the simulations, pay attention to the magnitude of the shock e_y (1% by default) to compare it to the magnitude of the response for assets b.
In all simulations, the persistent effect of the shock on the level of debt comes from the presence of a unit root.




In the modfile, add a preference for wealth term in the utility function and adjust the calibration of beta accordingly.







WarningCorrection





Parameters phi and eta are already predefined.
Add + phi*(1+b*q)^(-1/eta)/(c^(-1/sigma)) to the Euler equation and beta = 1/r - phi*(1+bbar*q)^(-1/eta)/(cbar^(-1/sigma)); in the definition of parameters
The simulation runs, only when the command check succeeds, in particular, when the steady-sate is satisfied. If it is not the case, you need to adjust the equations/calibration.
Result is in the one_agent_2.mod file.
Here we see that one unit root has disappeared, because pref. for wealth pins down equilibrium asset holdings.



6. Simulate the response to a temporary and a persistent shock. Given phi what is the effect of eta?






WarningCorrection





Now the savings response to transitory shock is mean reverting, while the response to a persistent shock is persistent and even increasing over time.
eta affects the long run savings level in response to a temporary income shock. (If curious you should be able to check it does not depend on phi).

title: A two agents model of inequalities.
text:
A two agents model
We now assume there are two agents: bottom and top earners. Top earners amount for a fraction \(\chi\) of the total population. Together they earn a fraction \(z\in[0,1]\) of the total production \(y\) which is an AR1 process as in the first part. The rest goes to the bottom earners.
Top earners can save by lending to bottom earners. We denote by \(B_t\) the total quantity of riskfree bonds, traded at \(q_t\). Note that debt per capita is \(\frac{B_t}{\chi}\) for top earners and \(\frac{B_t}{1-\chi}\) for bottom earners. Top earners have preference for wealth as in the first part, while bottom earners have standard preferences (with \(\varphi=0\))

Write down the budget equations for both agents. What are the new Euler equations? Check that it is consistent with the two_agents.mod modfile. What are the per capita variables?







WarningCorrection





Budget constraints are included in the modfile.
Per capita variables: c_t, c_b, b_t and b_b.




What is qualitatively the effect of a permanent redistributive shock? (simulate the model)







WarningCorrection





With increased income, top earners want to increase their asset holdings. This will happen since bottom earners are indifferent in the steady-state. During the transition however, we see a decrease in the interest rate (to convince borrower to accept a decreasing path of consumption over time).



Response to a redistributive shock

title: A two agents model of inequalities.
text:
Calibrating and simulating the model
The model in the modefile is pre-calibrated to match US data in 1983. Assume the model is in equilibrium for an initial level of debt \(B=0.91\) (which is the debt/gdp ratio in the us in 1983).
Taking \(\varphi=0.05\) as constant, for any given choice of \(\eta\), there is a unique value of \(\beta\) consistent with the equilibrium as in the one agent case.
Now we would like to calibrate the value of \(\eta\) so as to match the marginal propensity to save of top earners which was approximately 50% in 1983.
Since the two agents model is already calibrated for most variables, we reuse it rather than adapting the one agent model.

In the model two_agents.mod replace the Euler equation of bottom earner by q = 1/rbar. Justify why, from the top earners perspective, the model is now equivalent to a single agent model.







WarningCorrection





Interest rate is the only manifestation of bottom earners preferences in the model. If we set it equal to a constant, top earners are now facing an infinitely elastic demand of bonds as in the one agent case.



@. Use the modified model to compute the marginal propensity out of a permanent income shock after 6 periods. Choose parameter eta so that this m.p.s. is approximatively 50%.






WarningCorrection





Several options here. We can just make a persistent shock of size sig_z to the overall income \(y\) and compute the marginal propensity to save at different periods (variable mps). It must be normalized by the initial income level of top earners. We can then run computations for different values of \(eta\) until we find the desired value.
Modified model is two_agents_mps.mps. I found eta=0.6 to be a good calibration.




@dynare("two_agents_mps.mps")


What is the effect of a 10% permanent increase in inequalities? Over 30 years and in the long run?







WarningCorrection





Several options here. We can just make a persistent shock of size sig_z to the overall income \(y\) and compute the marginal propensity to save at different periods (variable mps). It must be normalized by the initial income level of top earners. We can then run computations for different values of \(eta\) until we find the desired value.
Modified model is two_agents_mps.mps. I found eta=0.6 to be a good calibration.

Small Open Economy
Start from the same rbc model. Assume the representative agent can save \(b^{\star}_t\) as foreign assets, remunerated at a constant interest rate \(r^{\star}-1\).
Write the new budget constraint for the representative household.
√А une date donn√©e t, le m√©nage √©pargne \(b^{\star}_t\) sous forme de titres √©trangers et re√Іoit le remboursement de son √©pargne de la p√©riode pr√©c√©dente \(b^{\star}_{t-1}\) plus les int√©r√™ts aff√©rents \((r^{\star} вИТ 1)b^{\star}_{t-1}\) . Par rapport au mod√®le de base, la contrainte budg√©taire en t devient donc :
\[c_t + i_t + b^ {\star}_t \leq w_t n_t + r_t k_{t-1} + r^ {\star} b^{\star}_{t-1}\]
and the lagrangian is:
\[\mathcal{L} = E_t \sum_{j=0}^\infty \beta^j\left[\log c_{t+j} + \chi \dfrac{(1-n_{t+j})^{1-\eta}}{1-\eta} \right.\]
\[+\lambda_{t+j} (w_{t+j}n_{t+j} + r_{t+j}k_{t+j-1} - c_{t+j}-k_{t+j} + (1-\delta)k_{t+j-1} + r^* b^*_{t+j-1} - b^*_{t+j} )\Bigg]\]
Write the new optimality condition.
Il faut ajouter au mod√®le la condition dвАЩoptimalit√© par rapport √† \(b^{\star}_t\) : le m√©nage repr√©sentatif choisit le niveau dвАЩ√©pargne en titres √©trangers qui maximise son utilit√© intertemporelle sous contrainte. On trouve :
\[ -\lambda_t + \beta E_t[\lambda_{t+1}r^*]=0\] soit : \[ \frac{1}{c_t}=\beta r^* E_t\left[\frac{1}{c_{t+1}} \right]\]
What is the long run constraint on interest rate \(r^{\star}\)?
√А lвАЩ√©tat stationnaire, cette √©quation implique \(r^{\star}= \frac{1}{\beta}\) . Bien que \(r^{\star}\) repr√©sente un taux dвАЩint√©r√™t d√©cid√© √† lвАЩ√©tranger, \(\frac{1}{\beta}\) est sa seule valeur compatible avec lвАЩexistence dвАЩun √©quilibre de long terme dans le mod√®le. En effet, il ne peut pas y avoir dвАЩopportunit√© dвАЩarbitrage √† lвАЩ√©quilibre : tous les placements ont la m√™me rentabilit√©.
Update the modfile (set \(\overline{b^{\star}}=0\))
Pour mettre √† jour le fichier .mod, il faut d√©clarer la nouvelle variable \(b^{\star}\) et le nouveau param√®tre \(r^\star\)? en lui donnant la valeur \(\frac{1}{\beta}\) . Il faut ajouter au mod√®le la condition dвАЩoptimalit√© ci-dessus et mettre √† jour lвАЩ√©quation donnant lвАЩ√©quilibre sur le march√© des biens. En effet, cette √©quation est obtenue en √©quilibre g√©n√©ral √† partir de la contrainte budg√©taire du m√©nage. En petite √©conomie ouverte, on a :
\[c_t + i_t + b^{\star}_t = w_t n_t + r_t k_{t-1} + r^{\star} b^{\star}_{t-1}\]
Or lвАЩentreprise, suppos√©e en concurrence parfaite, ne fait pas de profit. Donc \(y_t = w_t n_t + r_t k_{tвИТ1}\) et :
\[c_t + i_t + b^{\star}_t - r^{\star} b^{\star}_{t-1} = y_t\]
Ensuite, on remarque que le mod√®le statique ne d√©termine pas le niveau de \(b^{\star}\) √† lвАЩ√©tat stationnaire ; on choisit de le fixer √† 0 de sorte que lвАЩ√©tat stationnaire des autres variables du mod√®le ne soit pas affect√©. On ajoute donc \(b^{\star}=0\) dans le bloc steady_state_model;.
Print theoretical moments. Comment.
On voit que Dynare ne parvient pas √† calculer les moments (moyennes et variances) de certaines variables. De plus, les irfs ne reviennent pas tous √† 0.
Simulate the model over 100 periods. Comment.
Avec la commande check;, on voit que le mod√®le a une valeur propre de module √©gal √† 1 [PAS DANS Dynare Julia]. Dans Dynare, ceci ne provoque pas dвАЩerreur par rapport aux conditions de Blanchard et Kahn mais est probl√©matique : le mod√®le a une racine unitaire et nвАЩest donc pas stationnaire. En effet, il manque au mod√®le une force de rappel : si les m√©nages sont transitoirement incit√©s √©pargner plus sous forme de titres √©trangers, rien ne les incite ensuite de fa√Іon endog√®ne √† baisser cette √©pargne vers son niveau initial вАУ la r√©mun√©ration de cette √©pargne √©tant toujours la m√™me.
Assume the foreign interest rate depends on the amount of foreign assets
\[r^{\star}=\frac{1}{\beta} + exp(-\kappa b^{\star}_t) - 1\]
with \(\kappa=0.01\). How do you interpret the equation for \(r^{\star}_t\). How should you modify the model equations?
Plus le m√©nage d√©tient de titres √©trangers, moins ceux-ci sont r√©mun√©rateurs. Inversement, plus le m√©nage est endett√© vis-√†-vis de lвАЩ√©tranger \((b^{\star} < 0)\), plus les pr√™teurs √©trangers vont exiger un taux dвАЩ int√©r√™t √©lev√©. On peut donc interpr√©ter le terme \(exp(вИТ\kappa b^{\star}_t ) вИТ 1\) comme une prime de risque sur le taux dвАЩint√©r√™t √©tranger. Pour mettre √† jour le fichier .mod, il faut d√©clarer √† pr√©sent \(r^{\star}\) comme une variable et non un param√®tre, d√©clarer sa valeur √† lвАЩ√©tat stationnaire \(r^{\star} = \frac{1}{ќ≤}\) dans le bloc steady_state_model; et enfin ajouter lвАЩ√©quation ci-dessus dans le bloc model;.
Si on ne change pas les conditions dвАЩ Euler, cela revient √† supposer que les consommateurs/le gouvernment nвАЩinternalisent pas lвАЩ effet de leur emprunt sur le taux dвАЩ int√©r√™t.
Update the modfile and comment on the generated moments. Do they depend on the choice of \(\kappa\)?

title: First steps with Dynare and moment matching.

NoteObjectives





Using Dynare:

Using Dynare
Download the rbc modfile.

Open the RBC model and solve the model. Fix the mistakes in the modfile if any.

(if needed, download the correct model here )

Interpret the effect of a productivity shock. How does it depend on the productivity?
Bonus: open the same model with Dyno (see link on the course webpage). Compare the results.

Download US time series from the world bank for: real gdp, investment, consumption, hours worked.

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Quelle est la principale sp√©cificit√© de la mod√©lisation √©conomique ?

En √©conomie, nous mod√©lisons le comportement des agents √©conomiques en sp√©cifiant :

leur objectif \[\max_{c_t} E_t \sum_{s\geq t} \beta^s U(c_s)\] \[\max \pi_t\] \[\cdots\]
leurs contraintes (contrainte budg√©taire, environnement √©coвА¶)







En macro-√©conomie, cette approche a √©t√© d√©velopp√©e par les nouveaux classiques (Lucas, Sargent, Barro, Kydland & Prescott, etc.): on parle de micro-fondations.




Cela a des implications importantes :

les mod√®les macro donnent une part importante aux anticipations
les mod√®les macro doivent √™tre r√©solus



Dans de nombreux cas, il nвАЩexiste pas de forme ferm√©e pour la solution -> il faut des techniques num√©riques

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Dynare



1996 : Michel Juillard cr√©e un logiciel open source pour r√©soudre des mod√®les DSGE

DSGE : Dynamic Stochastic General Equilibrium

historiquement: extensions du mod√®le RBC
maintenant utilis√©s pour une large classe de mod√®les

g√©n√©ralement r√©solus autour dвАЩun √©tat stationnaire

AujourdвАЩhui, environ 10 contributeurs.

\(+\) des power users qui ont contribu√© au code




Il a √©t√© largement adopt√© :

premi√®res versions sous Gauss
puis Matlab/Octave/Scilab
derni√®re version en Julia

Dans ce cours, on utilise dyno рЯ¶Ц, une variante en python

compatible avec les modfiles dynare
syntax simplifi√©e + interface graphique







Michel Juillard

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Mod√®les DSGE dans les institutions
AujourdвАЩhui, la plupart des mod√®les DSGE d√©velopp√©s dans les institutions ont une version Dynare (FMI/GIMF, CE/Quest, BCE/, NYFed/FRBNY)

ils sont g√©n√©ralement bas√©s sur le mod√®le de taille interm√©diaire de Smets & Wouters (10 √©quations)
mais ils ont beaucoup grandi (>>100 √©quations)


Les institutions, port√©es par les chercheurs, diversifient leurs mod√®les

Mod√®les semi-structurels
Mod√®les dвАЩ√©quilibre g√©n√©ral calculable
Mod√®les de r√©seaux
Mod√®les √† agents
Mod√®les √† agents h√©t√©rog√®nes

text:
Le timing des √©quations: examples
Avec les conventions de timing de Dynare :

√Йcrire la fonction de production dans le mod√®le RBC
√Йcrire la loi de mouvement du capital \(k\), avec un taux de d√©pr√©ciation \(\delta\) et lвАЩinvestissement \(i\)

quand le capital est-il connu ?
quand lвАЩinvestissement est-il connu ?

Ajouter un choc multiplicatif dвАЩefficacit√© de lвАЩinvestissement \(\chi_t\). Supposer quвАЩil suit un \(AR1\) pilot√© par lвАЩinnovation \(\eta_t\) et lвАЩautocorr√©lation \(\rho_{\chi}\)

comment √©crivez-vous la loi de mouvement du capital ?

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Mod√®le
Une repr√©sentation tr√®s concise dвАЩun mod√®le
\[\mathbb{E}_t \left[ f(y_{t+1}, y_t, y_{t-1}, \epsilon_t) \right]= 0\] 

Le probl√®me :

\(y_t\in\mathbb{R}^n\) : le vecteur des variables endog√®nes
\(\epsilon_t\in\mathbb{R}^{n_e}\) : le vecteur des variables exog√®nes

on suppose que \(\epsilon_t\) est un processus gaussien centr√©

\(f: \mathbb{R}^n\rightarrow \mathbb{R}^n\) : les √©quations du mod√®le




La solution :

\(g\) telle que \(\forall t, y_t = g(y_{t-1},\epsilon_t)\)
\(g\) est la r√®gle de d√©cision / politique
elle caract√©rise la dynamique du mod√®le r√©solu



La situation est diff√©rente lorsquвАЩon r√©alise une simulation en pr√©vision parfaite.

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Le syst√®me implicite
En rempla√Іant la solution \[y_t = \color{red}{g}(y_{t-1},\epsilon_t)\] dans le syst√®me \[\mathbb{E}_t \left[ f(y_{t+1}, y_t, y_{t-1}, \epsilon_t) \right]= 0\]
on obtient :
\[\mathbb{E}_t \left[ f(\color{red}{g}(\color{red}{g}(y_{t-1},\epsilon_{t}),\epsilon_{t+1}), \color{red}{g}(y_{t-1},\epsilon_t), y_{t-1}, \epsilon_t) \right]= 0\]
CвАЩest une √©quation qui d√©finit implicitement la fonction \(\color{red}{g}()\)

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
LвАЩespace dвАЩ√©tat
\[\mathbb{E}_t \left[ f(\color{red}{g}(\color{red}{g}(y_{t-1},\epsilon_{t}),\epsilon_{t+1}), \color{red}{g}(y_{t-1},\epsilon_t), y_{t-1}, \epsilon_t) \right]= 0\]
Dans cette expression, \(y_{t-1},\epsilon_t\) est lвАЩespace dвАЩ√©tat :

il contient toute lвАЩinformation disponible en \(t\) pour pr√©dire lвАЩ√©volution future de \((y_s)_{s\geq t}\)


En supprimant les indices temporels, lвАЩ√©quation doit √™tre satisfaite pour toute r√©alisation de \((y,\epsilon)\) \[\forall (y,\epsilon)\  \Phi(\color{red}{g})(y,\epsilon) = \mathbb{E}_{\epsilon'} \left[ f(\color{red}{g}(\color{red}{g}(y,\epsilon),\epsilon'), \color{red}{g}(y,\epsilon), y, \epsilon) \right]= 0\]
CвАЩest une √©quation fonctionnelle \(\Phi(\color{red}{g})=0\)

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Chocs anticip√©s
Approximation du premier ordre :

Supposer \(|\epsilon|<<1\),\(|\epsilon'|<<1\)

Effectuer un d√©veloppement de Taylor par rapport au choc futur :
\[\begin{align}
& & \mathbb{E}_{\color{green}{\epsilon'}} \left[ f(g(g(y,\epsilon),\color{green}{\epsilon'}), g(y,\epsilon), y, \epsilon) \right]  \\
= & &  \mathbb{E}_{\color{green}{\epsilon'}}\left[ f(g(g(y,\epsilon),0), g(y,\epsilon), y, \epsilon) \right] \\
  & & +  \mathbb{E}_{\color{green}{\epsilon'}} \left[ f^{\prime}_{y_{t+1}}(g(g(y,\epsilon),0), g(y,\epsilon), y, \epsilon) g^{\prime}_{\epsilon} \color{green}{\epsilon'}\right] + o(\epsilon^{\prime}) \\
\approx & & f(g(g(y,\epsilon),0), g(y,\epsilon), y, \epsilon)
\end{align}\]

On utilise le fait que \(\mathbb{E}\left[ \epsilon^{\prime}\right] = 0\).
Au premier ordre, les chocs anticip√©s ne jouent aucun r√іle.
Pour capturer les comportements de pr√©caution (comme les primes de risque), il faut augmenter lвАЩordre dвАЩapproximation.

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Perturbation du premier ordre
Il nous reste le syst√®me :
\[F(y,\epsilon) = f(g(g(y,\epsilon),0), g(y,\epsilon), y, \epsilon) = 0\]
Une variante du th√©or√®me des fonctions implicites donne alors lвАЩexistence dвАЩune premi√®re approximation de \(g\) :
\[g(y,\epsilon) = \overline{y} + g^{\prime}_{y} (y-\overline{y}) + g^{\prime}_e \epsilon_t \]

Les quantit√©s inconnues \(g^{\prime}_y\), et \(g^{\prime}_e\) sont obtenues avec la m√©thode des coefficients ind√©termin√©s. On injecte la premi√®re approximation dans le syst√®me et on √©crit les conditions \[F^{\prime}_y(\overline{y}, 0) = 0\] \[F^{\prime}_\epsilon(\overline{y}, 0) = 0\]

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Calcul de \(g^{\prime}_y\)
Rappel du syst√®me : \[F(y,\epsilon) = f(g(g(y,\epsilon),0), g(y,\epsilon), y, \epsilon) = 0\]
On a \[F^{\prime}_y(\overline{y}, 0) = f^{\prime}_{y_{t+1}} g^{\prime}_y g^{\prime}_y + f^{\prime}_{y_{t}} g^{\prime}_y + f^{\prime}_{y_{t-1}} = 0 \]

\(g^{\prime}_y\) est la solution dвАЩune √©quation de Riccati sp√©cifique \[A X^2 + B X + C\] o√є \(A,B,C\) et \(X=g^{\prime}_y\) sont des matrices carr√©es \(\in \mathbb{R}^n \times \mathbb{R}^n\)

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Mod√®le d√©terministe du premier ordre
Faisons une pause pour observer le mod√®le d√©terministe du premier ordre : \[A X^2 + B X + C\]
DвАЩapr√®s notre intuition en dimension 1, on sait quвАЩil doit y avoir plusieurs solutions

comment les trouver ?
comment s√©lectionner les bonnes ?

En lвАЩabsence de chocs, la dynamique du mod√®le est donn√©e par \[y_t = X y_{t-1}\]
Quelle est la condition pour que le mod√®le soit stationnaire ?

-> la plus grande valeur propre de \(X\) doit √™tre inf√©rieure √† 1

D√©velopper lвАЩintuition en dimension 1.

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Multiplicit√© des solutions
On peut montrer que le syst√®me est associ√© √† \(2 n\) valeurs propres g√©n√©ralis√©es :
\[|\lambda_1| \leq \cdots \leq |\lambda_{2 n}|\]
Pour chaque choix \(C\) de \(n\) valeurs propres (\(|C|=n\)), une solution r√©cursive sp√©cifique \(X_C\) peut √™tre construite. Elle a pour valeurs propres \(C\).

Cela donne au moins \(\left(\begin{matrix} 2 n \\ n \end{matrix}\right)\) combinaisons diff√©rentes.


Un mod√®le est bien d√©fini lorsquвАЩil existe exactement une solution non divergente.
Ceci est √©quivalent √† :
\[|\lambda_1| \leq \cdots \leq |\lambda_{n}| \leq 1 < |\lambda_{n+1}| \leq \cdots \leq |\lambda_{2 n}|\]

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Exemple 1
Inflation orient√©e vers le futur :
\[\pi_t = \alpha \pi_{t+1}\] avec \(\alpha<1\).
Le mod√®le est-il bien d√©fini ?

On peut r√©√©crire le syst√®me :
\[\alpha \pi_{t+1} - \pi_t + 0 \pi_{t-1}  = 0\]
ou
\[\pi_{t+1} - \left(\frac{1}{\alpha} + 0 \right)\pi_t + \left(\frac{1}{\alpha} 0\right) \pi_{t-1} = 0\]


Les valeurs propres g√©n√©ralis√©es sont \(0\leq 1 < \frac{1}{\alpha}\).


La solution stable unique est \(\pi_t=0 \pi_{t-1}\)

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Exemple 2
√Йquation dвАЩaccumulation de dette par un agent rationnel :
\[b_{t+1} - (1+\frac{1}{\beta}) b_t + \frac{1}{\beta} b_{t-1} = 0\]
Le mod√®le est-il bien d√©fini ?

Deux valeurs propres g√©n√©ralis√©es \(\lambda_1=1 < \lambda_2=\frac{1}{\beta}\)


La solution unique non divergente est \(b_t = b_{t-1}\).

cвАЩest une racine unitaire : toute d√©viation initiale de \(b_{t-1}\) a des effets persistants

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Exemple 3
Processus de productivit√© : \[z_t = \rho z_{t-1}\] avec \(\rho<1\) : bien d√©fini

Dans ce cas, il existe une valeur propre infinie cach√©e \(\infty\) associ√©e √† \(z_{t+1}\).


Pour voir pourquoi, consid√©rer le syst√®me associ√© aux valeurs propres \(m\) et \(\rho\) : \[z_{t+1} - (m+\rho) z_t + m \rho z_{t-1} = 0\]
\[\frac{1}{m} z_{t+1} - (1+\frac{\rho}{m}) z_t + \rho z_{t-1} = 0\]
Ce qui correspond au mod√®le initial quand \(m=\infty\)


Les valeurs propres g√©n√©ralis√©es sont \(\lambda_1 = \rho \leq 1 < \lambda_2 = \infty\)
Plus g√©n√©ralement, toute variable qui nвАЩappara√Ѓt pas en \(t+1\) cr√©e une valeur propre g√©n√©ralis√©e infinie.

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Un crit√®re de bonne d√©finition
En regardant √† nouveau la liste des valeurs propres, on met de c√іt√© les valeurs infinies.
Le mod√®le est bien sp√©cifi√© ssi on peut ordonner les valeurs propres comme suit :
\[|\lambda_1| \leq \cdots \leq |\lambda_{n}| \leq 1 < |\lambda_{n+1}| \leq \cdots  |\lambda_{n+k}| \leq \underbrace{|\lambda_{n+k+1}| \cdots \leq |\lambda_{2 n}|}_{\text{valeurs propres infinies}}\]






Crit√®re de Blanchard-Kahn


Le mod√®le satisfait le crit√®re de Blanchard-Kahn si le nombre de valeurs propres sup√©rieures √† un est exactement √©gal au nombre de variables apparaissant en \(t+1\).
Dans ce cas, le mod√®le est bien d√©fini.
Dans Dynare, la r√©solution √©choue si le crit√®re nвАЩest pas satisfait. La commande check en plus de v√©rifier lвАЩ√©tat stationnaire, v√©rifie aussi les conditions de BK.

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Calcul de la solution
Il existe plusieurs m√©thodes classiques pour calculer la solution de lвАЩ√©quation alg√©brique de Riccati : \[A X^2+ B X + C=0\]

d√©composition qz

traditionnellement utilis√©e dans la litt√©rature DSGE depuis Chris Sims
un peu contre-intuitive

r√©duction cyclique

nouveau d√©faut dans dynare, mieux adapt√©e aux grands mod√®les

it√©ration temporelle lin√©aire cf @sec:linear_time_iteration

conceptuellement tr√®s simple

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Calcul de \(g^{\prime}_{e}\)
Maintenant que nous avons \(g^{\prime}_y\), comment obtenir \(g^{\prime}_{e}\) ?
Rappel : \[F(y,\epsilon) = f(g(g(y,\epsilon),0), g(y,\epsilon), y, \epsilon) = 0\]
On a \[F^{\prime}_e(\overline{y}, 0) =  f^{\prime}_{y_{t+1}}  g^{\prime}_y  g^{\prime}_e + f^{\prime}_{y_{t}} g^{\prime}_e + f^{\prime}_{\epsilon_t} = 0 \]
Maintenant cвАЩest facile :
\[g^{\prime}_e = - (f^{\prime}_{y_{t+1}}  g^{\prime}_y + f^{\prime}_{y_{t}} )^{-1}  f^{\prime}_{\epsilon_t} = 0 \]

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
La solution du mod√®le
Le r√©sultat de la r√©solution du mod√®le : \[y_t = g_y y_{t-1} + g_e \epsilon_t\]
CвАЩest un AR1, pilot√© par le choc exog√®ne \(\epsilon_t\).

Comme cвАЩest une structure bien connue, on peut analyser le mod√®le avec

des fonctions de r√©ponse impulsionnelle
des simulations stochastiques



Puis, pour comparer le mod√®le aux donn√©es, on calcule

les moments implicites :

covariances, autocorr√©lation

la vraisemblance

Optimiser lвАЩajustement aux donn√©es sвАЩappelle lвАЩestimation du mod√®le

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Que faire avec la solution
La solution dвАЩun mod√®le trouv√©e par Dynare a une forme particuli√®rement simple : un AR1

\(y_t = X y_{t-1} + Y \epsilon_t\)
o√є les covariances \(\Sigma\) de \(\epsilon_t\) peuvent √™tre choisies par le mod√©lisateur


Avec cette solution, on peut (cf prochain TD)

calculer des moments (conditionnels et inconditionnels)
r√©aliser des simulations stochastiques, fonctions de r√©ponse impulsionnelle

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Aller plus loin
Amener le mod√®le aux donn√©es avec Dynare

вАЬestimerвАЭ le mod√®le : calculer la vraisemblance dвАЩune solution et la maximiser en choisissant les bons param√®tres
вАЬidentifierвАЭ les chocs dans les donn√©es

Autres fonctions

approximation dвАЩordre sup√©rieur
simulations en pr√©vision parfaite (non lin√©aires)
plan de Ramsey
politique discr√©tionnaire

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:

De nombreux mod√®les

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
It√©ration temporelle lin√©aire
Rappel du syst√®me √† r√©soudre : \[F(y,\epsilon) = f(g(g(y,\epsilon),0), g(y,\epsilon), y, \epsilon) = 0\]
mais supposons maintenant que les r√®gles de d√©cision aujourdвАЩhui et demain soient diff√©rentes :

aujourdвАЩhui : \(y_t = g(y_{t-1}, \epsilon_t) = \overline{y} + X y_{t-1} + g_y \epsilon_t\)
demain : \(y_{t+1} = \tilde{g}(y_t, \epsilon_{t+1}) = \overline{y} + \tilde{X} y_{t-1} + \tilde{g}_y \epsilon_t\)

LвАЩ√©quation de Riccati sвАЩ√©crit alors :
\[A \tilde{X} X + B X + C = 0\]

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
It√©ration temporelle lin√©aire (2)
LвАЩalgorithme dвАЩit√©ration temporelle lin√©aire consiste √† r√©soudre la r√®gle de d√©cision \(X\) aujourdвАЩhui en fonction de la r√®gle de d√©cision demain \(\tilde{X}\).
Cela correspond √† la formule simple :
\[X = -(A\tilde{X} + B)^{-1} C\]
Et lвАЩalgorithme complet peut √™tre d√©crit ainsi :

choisir \(X_0\)
pour tout \(X_n\), calculer \(X_{n+1} = T(X_n) = -(A X_n + B)^{-1} C\)

r√©p√©ter jusquвАЩ√† convergence

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
It√©ration temporelle lin√©aire (3)
On peut montrer quвАЩen partant dвАЩune valeur initiale al√©atoire, lвАЩalgorithme dвАЩit√©ration temporelle lin√©aire converge vers la solution \(X\) de plus petit module :
\[\underbrace{|\lambda_1| \leq \cdots  \leq |\lambda_n|}_{\text{Valeurs propres s√©lectionn√©es}} \leq |\lambda_{n+1}|\cdots \leq |\lambda_{2n}|\]
Autrement dit, il trouve la bonne solution lorsque le mod√®le est bien sp√©cifi√©.
Comment v√©rifier quвАЩil est bien sp√©cifi√© ?

\(\lambda_n\) est la plus grande valeur propre de la solution \(X\)
et pour \(\lambda_{n+1}\) ?

\(\frac{1}{\lambda_{n+1}}\) est la plus grande valeur propre de \((AX+B)^{-1}A\)

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
It√©ration temporelle lin√©aire (4)
D√©finir \[M(\lambda) = A\lambda^2 + B \lambda + C\]
Pour toute solution \(X\), \(M(\lambda)\) peut √™tre factoris√©e comme : 1
\[M(\lambda)=(\lambda A + A X + B)(\lambda I -X)\]
et
\[det(M(\lambda)) = \underbrace{det(\lambda A + A X + B)}_{Q(\lambda)}det(\lambda I -X)\]
Par construction, \(Q(\lambda)\) est un polyn√іme dont les racines sont celles qui ne sont pas s√©lectionn√©es par la solution, c.-√†-d.¬†\(\Lambda\setminus Sp(X)\).
Cas particulier du th√©or√®me de Bezout. Facile √† v√©rifier dans ce cas

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
It√©ration temporelle lin√©aire (5)
Pour \(\lambda \neq 0\) on a :
\[\lambda \in Sp((A X +B )^{-1} A)\] \[\iff\det( (A X +B )^{-1})A - I\lambda )=0\] \[\iff\det( \frac{1}{\lambda} A - I (A X +B )  )=0\] \[\iff Q(\frac{1}{\lambda})=0\] \[\iff \frac{1}{\lambda} \in G \setminus Sp(X)\]
En mots, \((AX+B)^{-1}\) contient toutes les valeurs propres qui ont √©t√© rejet√©es par la s√©lection de \(X\).
En particulier, \(\rho((AX+B)^{-1})A)=1/\min(G\setminus Sp(X))\)

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Quelle est la principale sp√©cificit√© de la mod√©lisation √©conomique ?

En √©conomie, nous mod√©lisons le comportement des agents √©conomiques en sp√©cifiant :

leur objectif \[\max_{c_t} E_t \sum_{s\geq t} \beta^s U(c_s)\] \[\max \pi_t\] \[\cdots\]
leurs contraintes (contrainte budg√©taire, environnement √©coвА¶)






. . .

En macro-√©conomie, cette approche a √©t√© d√©velopp√©e par les nouveaux classiques (Lucas, Sargent, Barro, Kydland & Prescott, etc.): on parle de micro-fondations.

. . .

Cela a des implications importantes :

les mod√®les macro donnent une part importante aux anticipations
les mod√®les macro doivent √™tre r√©solus



Dans de nombreux cas, il nвАЩexiste pas de forme ferm√©e pour la solution -> il faut des techniques num√©riques

text:

Avec les conventions de timing de Dynare :

√Йcrire la fonction de production dans le mod√®le RBC
√Йcrire la loi de mouvement du capital \(k\), avec un taux de d√©pr√©ciation \(\delta\) et lвАЩinvestissement \(i\)

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Mod√®le
Une repr√©sentation tr√®s concise dвАЩun mod√®le
\[\mathbb{E}_t \left[ f(y_{t+1}, y_t, y_{t-1}, \epsilon_t) \right]= 0\] 

Le probl√®me :

\(y_t\in\mathbb{R}^n\) : le vecteur des variables endog√®nes
\(\epsilon_t\in\mathbb{R}^{n_e}\) : le vecteur des variables exog√®nes

on suppose que \(\epsilon_t\) est un processus gaussien centr√©

\(f: \mathbb{R}^n\rightarrow \mathbb{R}^n\) : les √©quations du mod√®le




La solution :

\(g\) telle que \(\forall t, y_t = g(y_{t-1},\epsilon_t)\)
\(g\) est la r√®gle de d√©cision / politique
elle caract√©rise la dynamique du mod√®le r√©solu



La situation est diff√©rente lorsquвАЩon r√©alise une simulation en pr√©vision parfaite.

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Le syst√®me implicite
En rempla√Іant la solution \[y_t = \color{red}{g}(y_{t-1},\epsilon_t)\] dans le syst√®me \[\mathbb{E}_t \left[ f(y_{t+1}, y_t, y_{t-1}, \epsilon_t) \right]= 0\]
on obtient :
\[\mathbb{E}_t \left[ f(\color{red}{g}(\color{red}{g}(y_{t-1},\epsilon_{t}),\epsilon_{t+1}), \color{red}{g}(y_{t-1},\epsilon_t), y_{t-1}, \epsilon_t) \right]= 0\]
CвАЩest une √©quation qui d√©finit implicitement la fonction \(\color{red}{g}()\)

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
LвАЩespace dвАЩ√©tat
\[\mathbb{E}_t \left[ f(\color{red}{g}(\color{red}{g}(y_{t-1},\epsilon_{t}),\epsilon_{t+1}), \color{red}{g}(y_{t-1},\epsilon_t), y_{t-1}, \epsilon_t) \right]= 0\]
Dans cette expression, \(y_{t-1},\epsilon_t\) est lвАЩespace dвАЩ√©tat :

il contient toute lвАЩinformation disponible en \(t\) pour pr√©dire lвАЩ√©volution future de \((y_s)_{s\geq t}\)

. . .
En supprimant les indices temporels, lвАЩ√©quation doit √™tre satisfaite pour toute r√©alisation de \((y,\epsilon)\) \[\forall (y,\epsilon)\  \Phi(\color{red}{g})(y,\epsilon) = \mathbb{E}_{\epsilon'} \left[ f(\color{red}{g}(\color{red}{g}(y,\epsilon),\epsilon'), \color{red}{g}(y,\epsilon), y, \epsilon) \right]= 0\]
CвАЩest une √©quation fonctionnelle \(\Phi(\color{red}{g})=0\)

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Chocs anticip√©s
Approximation du premier ordre :

Supposer \(|\epsilon|<<1\),\(|\epsilon'|<<1\)

Effectuer un d√©veloppement de Taylor par rapport au choc futur :
\[\begin{align}
& & \mathbb{E}_{\color{green}{\epsilon'}} \left[ f(g(g(y,\epsilon),\color{green}{\epsilon'}), g(y,\epsilon), y, \epsilon) \right]  \\
= & &  \mathbb{E}_{\color{green}{\epsilon'}}\left[ f(g(g(y,\epsilon),0), g(y,\epsilon), y, \epsilon) \right] \\
  & & +  \mathbb{E}_{\color{green}{\epsilon'}} \left[ f^{\prime}_{y_{t+1}}(g(g(y,\epsilon),0), g(y,\epsilon), y, \epsilon) g^{\prime}_{\epsilon} \color{green}{\epsilon'}\right] + o(\epsilon^{\prime}) \\
\approx & & f(g(g(y,\epsilon),0), g(y,\epsilon), y, \epsilon)
\end{align}\]
. . .
On utilise le fait que \(\mathbb{E}\left[ \epsilon^{\prime}\right] = 0\).
Au premier ordre, les chocs anticip√©s ne jouent aucun r√іle.
Pour capturer les comportements de pr√©caution (comme les primes de risque), il faut augmenter lвАЩordre dвАЩapproximation.

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Perturbation du premier ordre
Il nous reste le syst√®me :
\[F(y,\epsilon) = f(g(g(y,\epsilon),0), g(y,\epsilon), y, \epsilon) = 0\]
Une variante du th√©or√®me des fonctions implicites donne alors lвАЩexistence dвАЩune premi√®re approximation de \(g\) :
\[g(y,\epsilon) = \overline{y} + g^{\prime}_{y} (y-\overline{y}) + g^{\prime}_e \epsilon_t \]
. . .
Les quantit√©s inconnues \(g^{\prime}_y\), et \(g^{\prime}_e\) sont obtenues avec la m√©thode des coefficients ind√©termin√©s. On injecte la premi√®re approximation dans le syst√®me et on √©crit les conditions \[F^{\prime}_y(\overline{y}, 0) = 0\] \[F^{\prime}_\epsilon(\overline{y}, 0) = 0\]

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Calcul de \(g^{\prime}_y\)
Rappel du syst√®me : \[F(y,\epsilon) = f(g(g(y,\epsilon),0), g(y,\epsilon), y, \epsilon) = 0\]
On a \[F^{\prime}_y(\overline{y}, 0) = f^{\prime}_{y_{t+1}} g^{\prime}_y g^{\prime}_y + f^{\prime}_{y_{t}} g^{\prime}_y + f^{\prime}_{y_{t-1}} = 0 \]
. . .
\(g^{\prime}_y\) est la solution dвАЩune √©quation de Riccati sp√©cifique \[A X^2 + B X + C\] o√є \(A,B,C\) et \(X=g^{\prime}_y\) sont des matrices carr√©es \(\in \mathbb{R}^n \times \mathbb{R}^n\)

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Mod√®le d√©terministe du premier ordre
Faisons une pause pour observer le mod√®le d√©terministe du premier ordre : \[A X^2 + B X + C\]
DвАЩapr√®s notre intuition en dimension 1, on sait quвАЩil doit y avoir plusieurs solutions

comment les trouver ?
comment s√©lectionner les bonnes ?

En lвАЩabsence de chocs, la dynamique du mod√®le est donn√©e par \[y_t = X y_{t-1}\]
Quelle est la condition pour que le mod√®le soit stationnaire ?
. . .
-> la plus grande valeur propre de \(X\) doit √™tre inf√©rieure √† 1

D√©velopper lвАЩintuition en dimension 1.

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Multiplicit√© des solutions
On peut montrer que le syst√®me est associ√© √† \(2 n\) valeurs propres g√©n√©ralis√©es :
\[|\lambda_1| \leq \cdots \leq |\lambda_{2 n}|\]
Pour chaque choix \(C\) de \(n\) valeurs propres (\(|C|=n\)), une solution r√©cursive sp√©cifique \(X_C\) peut √™tre construite. Elle a pour valeurs propres \(C\).
. . .
Cela donne au moins \(\left(\begin{matrix} 2 n \\ n \end{matrix}\right)\) combinaisons diff√©rentes.
. . .
Un mod√®le est bien d√©fini lorsquвАЩil existe exactement une solution non divergente.
Ceci est √©quivalent √† :
\[|\lambda_1| \leq \cdots \leq |\lambda_{n}| \leq 1 < |\lambda_{n+1}| \leq \cdots \leq |\lambda_{2 n}|\]

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Exemple 1
Inflation orient√©e vers le futur :
\[\pi_t = \alpha \pi_{t+1}\] avec \(\alpha<1\).
Le mod√®le est-il bien d√©fini ?
. . .
On peut r√©√©crire le syst√®me :
\[\alpha \pi_{t+1} - \pi_t + 0 \pi_{t-1}  = 0\]
ou
\[\pi_{t+1} - \left(\frac{1}{\alpha} + 0 \right)\pi_t + \left(\frac{1}{\alpha} 0\right) \pi_{t-1} = 0\]
. . .
Les valeurs propres g√©n√©ralis√©es sont \(0\leq 1 < \frac{1}{\alpha}\).
. . .
La solution stable unique est \(\pi_t=0 \pi_{t-1}\)

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Exemple 2
√Йquation dвАЩaccumulation de dette par un agent rationnel :
\[b_{t+1} - (1+\frac{1}{\beta}) b_t + \frac{1}{\beta} b_{t-1} = 0\]
Le mod√®le est-il bien d√©fini ?
. . .
Deux valeurs propres g√©n√©ralis√©es \(\lambda_1=1 < \lambda_2=\frac{1}{\beta}\)
. . .
La solution unique non divergente est \(b_t = b_{t-1}\).

cвАЩest une racine unitaire : toute d√©viation initiale de \(b_{t-1}\) a des effets persistants

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Exemple 3
Processus de productivit√© : \[z_t = \rho z_{t-1}\] avec \(\rho<1\) : bien d√©fini
. . .
Dans ce cas, il existe une valeur propre infinie cach√©e \(\infty\) associ√©e √† \(z_{t+1}\).
. . .
Pour voir pourquoi, consid√©rer le syst√®me associ√© aux valeurs propres \(m\) et \(\rho\) : \[z_{t+1} - (m+\rho) z_t + m \rho z_{t-1} = 0\]
\[\frac{1}{m} z_{t+1} - (1+\frac{\rho}{m}) z_t + \rho z_{t-1} = 0\]
Ce qui correspond au mod√®le initial quand \(m=\infty\)
. . .
Les valeurs propres g√©n√©ralis√©es sont \(\lambda_1 = \rho \leq 1 < \lambda_2 = \infty\)
Plus g√©n√©ralement, toute variable qui nвАЩappara√Ѓt pas en \(t+1\) cr√©e une valeur propre g√©n√©ralis√©e infinie.

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Un crit√®re de bonne d√©finition
En regardant √† nouveau la liste des valeurs propres, on met de c√іt√© les valeurs infinies.
Le mod√®le est bien sp√©cifi√© ssi on peut ordonner les valeurs propres comme suit :
\[|\lambda_1| \leq \cdots \leq |\lambda_{n}| \leq 1 < |\lambda_{n+1}| \leq \cdots  |\lambda_{n+k}| \leq \underbrace{|\lambda_{n+k+1}| \cdots \leq |\lambda_{2 n}|}_{\text{valeurs propres infinies}}\]






NoteCrit√®re de Blanchard-Kahn



Le mod√®le satisfait le crit√®re de Blanchard-Kahn si le nombre de valeurs propres sup√©rieures √† un est exactement √©gal au nombre de variables apparaissant en \(t+1\).
Dans ce cas, le mod√®le est bien d√©fini.
Dans Dynare, la r√©solution √©choue si le crit√®re nвАЩest pas satisfait. La commande check en plus de v√©rifier lвАЩ√©tat stationnaire, v√©rifie aussi les conditions de BK.

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Calcul de la solution
Il existe plusieurs m√©thodes classiques pour calculer la solution de lвАЩ√©quation alg√©brique de Riccati : \[A X^2+ B X + C=0\]

d√©composition qz

traditionnellement utilis√©e dans la litt√©rature DSGE depuis Chris Sims
un peu contre-intuitive

r√©duction cyclique

nouveau d√©faut dans dynare, mieux adapt√©e aux grands mod√®les

it√©ration temporelle lin√©aire cf @sec:linear_time_iteration

conceptuellement tr√®s simple

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Calcul de \(g^{\prime}_{e}\)
Maintenant que nous avons \(g^{\prime}_y\), comment obtenir \(g^{\prime}_{e}\) ?
Rappel : \[F(y,\epsilon) = f(g(g(y,\epsilon),0), g(y,\epsilon), y, \epsilon) = 0\]
On a \[F^{\prime}_e(\overline{y}, 0) =  f^{\prime}_{y_{t+1}}  g^{\prime}_y  g^{\prime}_e + f^{\prime}_{y_{t}} g^{\prime}_e + f^{\prime}_{\epsilon_t} = 0 \]
Maintenant cвАЩest facile :
\[g^{\prime}_e = - (f^{\prime}_{y_{t+1}}  g^{\prime}_y + f^{\prime}_{y_{t}} )^{-1}  f^{\prime}_{\epsilon_t} = 0 \]

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
La solution du mod√®le
Le r√©sultat de la r√©solution du mod√®le : \[y_t = g_y y_{t-1} + g_e \epsilon_t\]
CвАЩest un AR1, pilot√© par le choc exog√®ne \(\epsilon_t\).
. . .
Comme cвАЩest une structure bien connue, on peut analyser le mod√®le avec

des fonctions de r√©ponse impulsionnelle
des simulations stochastiques

. . .
Puis, pour comparer le mod√®le aux donn√©es, on calcule

les moments implicites :

covariances, autocorr√©lation

la vraisemblance

Optimiser lвАЩajustement aux donn√©es sвАЩappelle lвАЩestimation du mod√®le

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Que faire avec la solution
La solution dвАЩun mod√®le trouv√©e par Dynare a une forme particuli√®rement simple : un AR1

\(y_t = X y_{t-1} + Y \epsilon_t\)
o√є les covariances \(\Sigma\) de \(\epsilon_t\) peuvent √™tre choisies par le mod√©lisateur

. . .
Avec cette solution, on peut (cf prochain TD)

calculer des moments (conditionnels et inconditionnels)
r√©aliser des simulations stochastiques, fonctions de r√©ponse impulsionnelle

. . .

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Aller plus loin
Amener le mod√®le aux donn√©es avec Dynare

вАЬestimerвАЭ le mod√®le : calculer la vraisemblance dвАЩune solution et la maximiser en choisissant les bons param√®tres
вАЬidentifierвАЭ les chocs dans les donn√©es

Autres fonctions

approximation dвАЩordre sup√©rieur
simulations en pr√©vision parfaite (non lin√©aires)
plan de Ramsey
politique discr√©tionnaire
вА¶

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
√А venir

De nombreux mod√®les

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
It√©ration temporelle lin√©aire
Rappel du syst√®me √† r√©soudre : \[F(y,\epsilon) = f(g(g(y,\epsilon),0), g(y,\epsilon), y, \epsilon) = 0\]
mais supposons maintenant que les r√®gles de d√©cision aujourdвАЩhui et demain soient diff√©rentes :

aujourdвАЩhui : \(y_t = g(y_{t-1}, \epsilon_t) = \overline{y} + X y_{t-1} + g_y \epsilon_t\)
demain : \(y_{t+1} = \tilde{g}(y_t, \epsilon_{t+1}) = \overline{y} + \tilde{X} y_{t-1} + \tilde{g}_y \epsilon_t\)

LвАЩ√©quation de Riccati sвАЩ√©crit alors :
\[A \tilde{X} X + B X + C = 0\]

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
It√©ration temporelle lin√©aire (2)
LвАЩalgorithme dвАЩit√©ration temporelle lin√©aire consiste √† r√©soudre la r√®gle de d√©cision \(X\) aujourdвАЩhui en fonction de la r√®gle de d√©cision demain \(\tilde{X}\).
Cela correspond √† la formule simple :
\[X = -(A\tilde{X} + B)^{-1} C\]
Et lвАЩalgorithme complet peut √™tre d√©crit ainsi :

choisir \(X_0\)
pour tout \(X_n\), calculer \(X_{n+1} = T(X_n) = -(A X_n + B)^{-1} C\)

r√©p√©ter jusquвАЩ√† convergence

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
It√©ration temporelle lin√©aire (3)
On peut montrer quвАЩen partant dвАЩune valeur initiale al√©atoire, lвАЩalgorithme dвАЩit√©ration temporelle lin√©aire converge vers la solution \(X\) de plus petit module :
\[\underbrace{|\lambda_1| \leq \cdots  \leq |\lambda_n|}_{\text{Valeurs propres s√©lectionn√©es}} \leq |\lambda_{n+1}|\cdots \leq |\lambda_{2n}|\]
Autrement dit, il trouve la bonne solution lorsque le mod√®le est bien sp√©cifi√©.
Comment v√©rifier quвАЩil est bien sp√©cifi√© ?

\(\lambda_n\) est la plus grande valeur propre de la solution \(X\)
et pour \(\lambda_{n+1}\) ?

\(\frac{1}{\lambda_{n+1}}\) est la plus grande valeur propre de \((AX+B)^{-1}A\)

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
It√©ration temporelle lin√©aire (4)
D√©finir \[M(\lambda) = A\lambda^2 + B \lambda + C\]
Pour toute solution \(X\), \(M(\lambda)\) peut √™tre factoris√©e comme : 1
\[M(\lambda)=(\lambda A + A X + B)(\lambda I -X)\]
et
\[det(M(\lambda)) = \underbrace{det(\lambda A + A X + B)}_{Q(\lambda)}det(\lambda I -X)\]
Par construction, \(Q(\lambda)\) est un polyn√іme dont les racines sont celles qui ne sont pas s√©lectionn√©es par la solution, c.-√†-d.¬†\(\Lambda\setminus Sp(X)\).

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
It√©ration temporelle lin√©aire (5)
Pour \(\lambda \neq 0\) on a :
\[\lambda \in Sp((A X +B )^{-1} A)\] \[\iff\det( (A X +B )^{-1})A - I\lambda )=0\] \[\iff\det( \frac{1}{\lambda} A - I (A X +B )  )=0\] \[\iff Q(\frac{1}{\lambda})=0\] \[\iff \frac{1}{\lambda} \in G \setminus Sp(X)\]
En mots, \((AX+B)^{-1}\) contient toutes les valeurs propres qui ont √©t√© rejet√©es par la s√©lection de \(X\).
En particulier, \(\rho((AX+B)^{-1})A)=1/\min(G\setminus Sp(X))\)

title: R√©solution des mod√®les DSGE
text:
Footnotes


Cas particulier du th√©or√®me de Bezout. Facile √† v√©rifier dans ce casвЖ©пЄО

text:
bayesian estimation banks and financially constrained households and firms.

estimation: differentiate between - usual shock - new financial shocks (redistribution, credit squeezes asset price shocks) fin shocks explain recessions
idea: busimess cycles are financial rather than real
literature: banks as intermediaries between savers and borrowers. Recent contributions include Brunnermeier and Sannikov (2014), Angeloni and Faia (2013), Gerali et al.¬†(2010), Kiley and Sim (2011), Kollmann et al.¬†(2011), Meh and Moran (2010), Williamson (2012), and Van den Heuvel (2008). T

title: Macro II - Fluctuations - Part II
text:
CREDIT MARKET STRESS AND THE ECONOMY At times, banking and credit markets become stressed, increasing the cost of borrowing and reducing the availability of credit. Credit market stress has macroeconomic consequences: вАҐ Contributed importantly to the Great Depression of the 1930s вАҐ Helps explain why the recession that followed the global financial crisis of 2007- 2009 was so severe вАҐ Helps explain the persistence of even вАЬgarden-varietyвАЭ recessions вАҐ Helps explain the potency of monetary policy вАҐ Justifies a strong response to systemic financial crises when they happen and effective financial regulation to ensure that they happen less often
THE SIMPLE ECONOMICS OF LENDING
Lending to private borrowers typically involves imperfect and asymmetric information. Borrowers know more than lenders about their own financial capacity, plans, and risks. Because of asymmetric information, lenders face adverse selection (the riskiest borrowers have the strongest incentives to apply) and moral hazard (borrowers have insufficient incentive to take care). Banks and other lenders specialize in overcoming information problems through longer-term relationships, screening, monitoring, imposing restrictions on borrowers (covenants), and requiring collateral.
The external finance premium (EFP) is the all-in cost of a loan for a given borrower (including costs created by covenants and collateral requirements, etc.), less the safe rate of interest (for example, yields on government securities). The EFP reflects the lenderвАЩs cost of credit intermediation for that borrower. EFPs typically differ by borrower: A small business will have a higher EFP than a financially strong corporation. However, EFPs tend to move together as the economy changes. When we talk about вАЬthe EFP,вАЭ we mean the typical or average EFP across the economy. A higher EFP corresponds to more stressed credit markets.
Banks also must borrow from ultimate savers. For reasons explored by laureates Diamond and Dybvig, banks typically rely on short-term funds (deposits, uninsured commercial paper, or repurchase agreements). Lenders to banks like the liquidity (easy conversion to cash) of their short-term loans. However, reliance on short-term funding makes banks vulnerable to panics (runs), in which (uninsured) providers of short-term funding lose confidence and rush to withdraw their funds. Panics, or the threat of panics, make lenders more cautious (the flight to quality). They become less willing or able to use their accumulated information, relationships, etc. to extend credit to otherwise trustworthy borrowers.
A key insight is that the cost of lending (EFP) depends importantly on the financial health (net worth) of both borrowers and lenders. When the net worth of borrowers is high, borrowers can bear more of any losses, creating a financial cushion for lenders and incentivizing borrowers to take care. For example, banks prefer to extend credit to homebuyers who make large down payments. Lender net worth also matters. For example, banks with lots of capital and cash on hand available to absorb losses find it easier to attract funding and avoid panics.
The (average) EFP both affects and is affected by the state of the economy: 1) A higher EFP (more stressed credit markets) implies tighter credit standards and reduced lending, which slows the economy. 2) A weaker economy in turn reduces the financial health/net worth of both lenders and borrowers, raising the EFP.
EFP: external cost of funding (all-in cost of a loan for a borrower)
THE GREAT RECESSION The Great Recession of 2007-2009 was largely the result of credit disruptions. The financial system of 2007 looked very different from 1929. Commercial banks were no longer dominant. A large share of credit was provided by the вАЬshadow banking system,вАЭ for example, investment banks, mortgage companies, money market funds, off-balance-sheet investment vehicles. The shadow banking system, ineligible for deposit insurance or (normally) for Federal Reserve loans, nevertheless relied heavily on uninsured, short-term funding (commercial paper, repurchase agreements) and thus was vulnerable to runs.

NoteObjectives

run cells
basic types: numbers, strings, vectors, matrices
functions
simple plots

Simulate AR1 models

impulse response functions
stochastic simulations

conditional / unconditional moments

develop intuition about eigenvalues / ergodic distributions

title: Julia Basics & VAR Process
text:
Why Julia?
Developped at MIT on top of opensource technologies

linux / git / llvm

Syntax inspired by Matlab but:

more consistent
lots of features from high level languages

Everything is JIT-compiled

no interpreted vs compiled treadeoff -> very fast
most of the base library is written in Julia

Opensource/free + vibrant community

title: Opening Economy Extension
text:
NoteObjectives






Modify a modfile with a model extension.
Compare model solutions for different parameterizations.










TipModfiles














rbc_fixed.mod











rbc_open.mod











rbc_open_stationary.mod











rbc_open_stationary_kappa05.mod





No matching items




Uncomment the following lines to install needed packages (if not already present).

# uncomment the next lines, the first time you run
# import Pkg; Pkg.add(["DBnomics", "Dynare", "DataFrames"])


Small Open Economy
Start from the same rbc model. Assume the representative agent can save \(b^{\star}_t\) as foreign assets, remunerated at a constant interest rate \(r^{\star}-1\).
Write the new budget constraint for the representative household.






WarningCorrection





At given date t, the household saves \(b^{\star}_t\) in the foreigh country and receives repayment \(b^{\star}_{t-1}\) plus interests \((r^{\star} вИТ 1)b^{\star}_{t-1}\) . Starting from the RBC constraint, the new constraint becomes: \[c_t + i_t + b^ {\star}_t \leq w_t n_t + r_t k_{t-1} + r^ {\star} b^{\star}_{t-1}\]
and the lagrangian is:
\[\mathcal{L} = E_t \sum_{j=0}^\infty \beta^j\left[\log c_{t+j} + \chi \dfrac{(1-n_{t+j})^{1-\eta}}{1-\eta} \right.\]
\[+\lambda_{t+j} (w_{t+j}n_{t+j} + r_{t+j}k_{t+j-1} - c_{t+j}-k_{t+j} + (1-\delta)k_{t+j-1} + r^* b^*_{t+j-1} - b^*_{t+j} )\Bigg]\]



Write the new optimality condition.






WarningCorrection





We must add the optimality condition relative to \(b^{\star}_t\) : the representative household chooses amount of savings to maximise its intertemporal utility of consumption. We find :
\[ -\lambda_t + \beta E_t[\lambda_{t+1}r^*]=0\] or : \[ \frac{1}{c_t}=\beta r^* E_t\left[\frac{1}{c_{t+1}} \right]\]



What is the long run constraint on interest rate \(r^{\star}\)?






WarningCorrection





In the deterministic steady-state the euler equation implies \(r^{\star}= \frac{1}{\beta}\) . In principle \(r^{\star}\) is a a foreign interest rate determined by factors external to the country. Yet \(\frac{1}{\beta}\) is the only value it can take that is compatible with the existence of a long run steady-state. This can be interpreted as an arbitrage condition: \(\frac{1}{\beta}\) is the domestic intesrest rate. If it was lower/higher than world interest rate, there would be infinite capital flows from/to the country.
(in a deterministic setting the arbitrage principle implies that all investment opportunities must yield the same return)



Update the modfile (set \(\overline{b^{\star}}=0\))






WarningCorrection





To update the modfile, one must declare a new variable \(b^{\star}\) and the new parameter \(r^\star\). The latter is set to \(\frac{1}{\beta}\) . One must also add the above optimality condition and update the equation characterizing the equilibrium on the goods markets. That one comes from the budget constraint. Rewriting the budget constraint for a small open economy we have:
\[c_t + i_t + b^{\star}_t = w_t n_t + r_t k_{t-1} + r^{\star} b^{\star}_{t-1}\]
Since we assume competitive markets, the firm doesnвАЩt do any profit. We have \(y_t = w_t n_t + r_t k_{tвИТ1}\) and:
\[c_t + i_t + b^{\star}_t - r^{\star} b^{\star}_{t-1} = y_t\]
Next, we can observe that the static model doesnвАЩt pin down the steady-state level of \(b^{\star}\) ; we set it to 0, so that the steady-state values of all other variables ar unchanged. We thus add \(b^{\star}=0\) in the steady_state_model; block.




using Dynare

context = @dynare "rbc_open.mod";
# saving the result in context is useful for future comparison

We can see that Dynare cannot compute the moments (mean and standard deviation) for all variables. This is a property associated to the existence of a unit root: the ergodic distribution of this variables is undefined.
We say the model is non-stationary.



Simulate the model over 100 periods. Comment.






WarningCorrection





The simulation horizon is specified in the stoch_simul(irf=200, order=1) as the argument to the irf keyword. The result is stored in the rbc_open/graphs subfolder.
Note that dynare-matlab allows you to select which variables to plot by adding a list of variables after the command (as in stoch_simul(irf=200, order=1) y c i b). This functionality has not been ported yet to Dynare Julia which prints by default the 8 first variables. You can instead

reorder the variables in the declaration statement to list first the ones we want to print
make a custom plot with Plots.jl (we do in the last question)


Even with a unit root, the model can be solved and simulated.
Since the opening up of the economy, in response to the productivity shock capital flows in to finance a jump in investment.
The irfs show a persistent effect of the shock on the debt position, as well as most other variables.
When the savings of all households increase, there is no stabilizing force to bring them back to their original level.
Households and as a result the whole country behaves like a ricardian agent: they increase their consumption permanently by consuming the interests on their assets.
Since their consumption increase their consumption leisure tradeoff is also modified, which is why the labour supply is also permanently changed.
Since the return on capital must be kept equal to world interest rate (by the arbitrage condition), it returns to the steady-state after one period.



Assume the foreign interest rate depends on the amount of foreign assets
\[r^{\star}_t=\frac{1}{\beta} + exp(-\kappa b^{\star}_t) - 1\]
with \(\kappa=0.001\). How do you interpret the equation for \(r^{\star}_t\). How should you modify the model equations?






WarningCorrection





With this specification, when the representative housheold holds more assets, their return decrease. This could be a proxy for the fact that international markets have limited depth.
Conversely, when the domestic household become more indebted, international lenders require a higher interest rate. In that case, it could mean that international investors donвАЩt have infinitely deep pockets or interpreted as a higher risk premium.
The precise specification is also chosen so that it collapses to the \(r^{\star}=\frac{1}{\beta}\) when \(b^{\star}_t=0\). This allows us to keep essentially the same steady-state as before for all other variables.
To update the modfile, we need to declare \(r^{\star}\) as a variable not a parameter and move its steady-state value \(r^{\star} = \frac{1}{ќ≤}\) to the block steady_state_model;. Finally the above equation endogenizing the interest rate must augment the model; block.
Note that the Euler equation is unchanged by the addition of the endogenous interest rate. This is because the households (and as a result the representative household) does not internalize the effect of its borrowing/saving on the interest rate when it chooses its optimal decisions.

We observe rather high increase in the volatility of debt, but milder changes for capital, investment, output, labour, wagesвА¶ In particular the RBC moments (std dev of investment / consumption over std dev of production) move very little.
Another interesting exercise consists in comparing the impulse response functions obtained with \(\kappa=0.000\), \(\kappa=0.001\) and \(\kappa=0.005\) (see code below).
In the short run, the dynamic responses are very similar for all variables, except consumption which does not jump anymore to its long run trend as long as there is a small friction on the interest rate. Overall the addition of the friction slows down the adjustment of production variables to the productivty shock.
In the long run, the friction implies a return to the initial steady-state counteracting the short term effect of the exogenous shock.

Small Open Economy
Start from the same rbc model as in last tutorial session. Assume the representative agent can save \(b^{\star}_t\) as foreign assets, remunerated at a constant interest rate \(r^{\star}-1\).
Write the new budget constraint for the representative household.
Write the new optimality condition.
What is the long run constraint on interest rate \(r^{\star}\)?
Update the modfile (set \(\overline{b^{\star}}=0\))
Print theoretical moments. Comment.
Simulate the model over 100 periods. Comment.
Assume the foreign interest rate depends on the amount of foreign assets
\[r^{\star}_t=\frac{1}{\beta} + exp(-\kappa b^{\star}_t) - 1\]
with \(\kappa=0.001\). How do you interpret the equation for \(r^{\star}_t\). How should you modify the model equations?
Update the modfile and comment on the generated moments. Do they depend on the choice of \(\kappa\)?

title: A two agents model of inequalities.
section: Preference for wealth and marginal propensity to consume
text:
Preference for wealth and marginal propensity to consume
For now, we consider a single representative agent. She has the ability to buy a two periods bond, yielding 1 after one period. The price of the bond at any date is \(q\), hence its (riskfree) interest rate is \(r=1/q\).
Agent values consumption \(c_t\) and wealth \(b_t q_t\) so that she maximizes1:
\[\max \sum_t \beta^t \left( \frac{c_t^{1-\frac{1}{\sigma}}}{1-\frac{1}{\sigma}}+ \varphi \frac{ (1+b_t)^{1-\frac{1}{\eta}} } {1-\frac{1}{\eta}} \right)\]
under the budget constraint
\[c_t = y_t + b_{t-1} - b_t q_t\]
where \(y_t\) is exogenous income following AR1
\[(y_t-\overline{y})=\rho (y_{t-1}-\overline{y}) + \epsilon^y_t\]

using Dynare


Write down the optimality condition for debt holdings.







WarningCorrection





We get:
\[q_t = \beta \left(\frac{c_{t+1}}{c_{t}}\right)^{-\frac{1}{\sigma}}+\varphi \left(\frac{1 + b_t q_t}{c_{t}}\right)^{-\frac{1}{\sigma}}\]



What are the equations defining the deterministic equilibrium?






WarningCorrection





At the equilibium, the last equation becomes. \[q = \beta + \varphi \left(\frac{1 + b q}{c}\right)^{-\frac{1}{\sigma}}\]



3. Inspect and run one_agent.mod model. Show that there is a unit root. Can you interpret it?






WarningCorrection





The one_agent.mod model is a standard consumption saving model without preference for wealth.
In general, if the solution of a model is \(y_t = A y_{t-1}\) where \(y_t\) is the vector of variables, we have a unit root when one of the eigenvalues of A is equal to 1 in absolute terms.
In the specific case of the modfile, we can directly read the values of matrix \(A\) are given in the output of dynare (precisely it is a subset of \(A'\))
  Coefficients of approximate solution function (reduced form)

вФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФђвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФА
       вФВ y_t   c_t    b_t
вФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФА
  ѕХ(b) вФВ 0.0  0.0385  1.0
 e_y_t вФВ 0.1  0.0038  0.1
вФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФівФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФАвФА
The rows correspond to the (predetermined) states. Non predetermined variables do not appear as rows (or equivalently with zeros everywhere).
Here we see that \(b_t\) is the only endogenous state. We also see that its dynamic is given by:
\[b_t = 1.0 b_{t-1} + 0.1 e_{y,t}\]
In other words, the dynamic of \(b\) is an AR1 with perfect persistence.
This behaviour (like in an open economy) comes from the fact that any steady-state level of debt is feasible (in a deterministic model). Hence shocks can affect the steady-state permanently.

What is the consumption response to a temporary income shock? To a permanent one? (with autocorrelation \(\rho=0.9\) and \(\rho=1.0\))







WarningCorrection





Change the income equation:

for a temporary shock remove the zero to get: y = ybar + e_y
For a persistent one, define a new parameter in the parameters line initialized to rho=0.9 and change the equation to y - ybar = rho*(y(-1) - ybar) + e_y. In the case rho=1.0 note the apparition of a second unit root.

In the simulations, pay attention to the magnitude of the shock e_y (1% by default) to compare it to the magnitude of the response for assets b.
In all simulations, the persistent effect of the shock on the level of debt comes from the presence of a unit root.




In the modfile, add a preference for wealth term in the utility function and adjust the calibration of beta accordingly.







WarningCorrection





Parameters phi and eta are already predefined.
Add + phi*(1+b*q)^(-1/eta)/(c^(-1/sigma)) to the Euler equation and beta = 1/r - phi*(1+bbar*q)^(-1/eta)/(cbar^(-1/sigma)); in the definition of parameters
The simulation runs, only when the command check succeeds, in particular, when the steady-sate is satisfied. If it is not the case, you need to adjust the equations/calibration.
Result is in the one_agent_2.mod file.
Here we see that one unit root has disappeared, because pref. for wealth pins down equilibrium asset holdings.



6. Simulate the response to a temporary and a persistent shock. Given phi what is the effect of eta?






WarningCorrection





Now the savings response to transitory shock is mean reverting, while the response to a persistent shock is persistent and even increasing over time.
eta affects the long run savings level in response to a temporary income shock. (If curious you should be able to check it does not depend on phi).

title: A two agents model of inequalities.
text:
A two agents model
We now assume there are two agents: bottom and top earners. Top earners amount for a fraction \(\chi\) of the total population. Together they earn a fraction \(z\in[0,1]\) of the total production \(y\) which is an AR1 process as in the first part. The rest goes to the bottom earners.
Top earners can save by lending to bottom earners. We denote by \(B_t\) the total quantity of riskfree bonds, traded at \(q_t\). Note that debt per capita is \(\frac{B_t}{\chi}\) for top earners and \(\frac{B_t}{1-\chi}\) for bottom earners. Top earners have preference for wealth as in the first part, while bottom earners have standard preferences (with \(\varphi=0\))

Write down the budget equations for both agents. What are the new Euler equations? Check that it is consistent with the two_agents.mod modfile. What are the per capita variables?







WarningCorrection





Budget constraints are included in the modfile.
Per capita variables: c_t, c_b, b_t and b_b.




What is qualitatively the effect of a permanent redistributive shock? (simulate the model)







WarningCorrection





With increased income, top earners want to increase their asset holdings. This will happen since bottom earners are indifferent in the steady-state. During the transition however, we see a decrease in the interest rate (to convince borrower to accept a decreasing path of consumption over time).



Response to a redistributive shock

title: A two agents model of inequalities.
text:
Calibrating and simulating the model
The model in the modefile is pre-calibrated to match US data in 1983. Assume the model is in equilibrium for an initial level of debt \(B=0.91\) (which is the debt/gdp ratio in the us in 1983).
Taking \(\varphi=0.05\) as constant, for any given choice of \(\eta\), there is a unique value of \(\beta\) consistent with the equilibrium as in the one agent case.
Now we would like to calibrate the value of \(\eta\) so as to match the marginal propensity to save of top earners which was approximately 50% in 1983.
Since the two agents model is already calibrated for most variables, we reuse it rather than adapting the one agent model.

In the model two_agents.mod replace the Euler equation of bottom earner by q = 1/rbar. Justify why, from the top earners perspective, the model is now equivalent to a single agent model.







WarningCorrection





Interest rate is the only manifestation of bottom earners preferences in the model. If we set it equal to a constant, top earners are now facing an infinitely elastic demand of bonds as in the one agent case.



@. Use the modified model to compute the marginal propensity out of a permanent income shock after 6 periods. Choose parameter eta so that this m.p.s. is approximatively 50%.






WarningCorrection





Several options here. We can just make a persistent shock of size sig_z to the overall income \(y\) and compute the marginal propensity to save at different periods (variable mps). It must be normalized by the initial income level of top earners. We can then run computations for different values of \(eta\) until we find the desired value.
Modified model is two_agents_mps.mps. I found eta=0.6 to be a good calibration.




@dynare("two_agents_mps.mps")


What is the effect of a 10% permanent increase in inequalities? Over 30 years and in the long run?







WarningCorrection





Several options here. We can just make a persistent shock of size sig_z to the overall income \(y\) and compute the marginal propensity to save at different periods (variable mps). It must be normalized by the initial income level of top earners. We can then run computations for different values of \(eta\) until we find the desired value.
Modified model is two_agents_mps.mps. I found eta=0.6 to be a good calibration.
